_comment: Guide metadata. Bodies live in content/guides/{slug}.html. Order here is publication order. insurance_cta places the Profitise comparison component at the end of the guide.

guides.title: The 2026 wind mitigation form explained: what changed on OIR-B1-1802
guides.description: Florida revised its wind mitigation inspection form effective April 1, 2026, the first major update in over a decade. What the new documentation rules mean for your discounts.

guides.title: My Safe Florida Home application walkthrough for the current cycle
guides.description: Step by step through the My Safe Florida Home application: eligibility rules, priority groups, the free inspection, the matching grant, and what not to do before approval.

guides.title: The My Safe Florida Home backlog: why homeowners wait, and what the 2026-27 budget does
guides.description: Why the inspection-to-grant queue exists, how priority groups order the line, and what the budget passed in May 2026 is meant to fix.

guides.title: Wind mitigation inspection cost and what happens during one
guides.description: What a Florida wind mitigation inspection costs, how long it takes, what the inspector documents, and why the 2026 form made the visit more thorough.

guides.title: Roof shape credits: hip vs gable on the wind mitigation form
guides.description: Why a hip roof earns the strongest shape credit on Florida's wind mitigation form, how roof shape is measured, and what you can and cannot do about it.

guides.title: Roof-to-wall attachment: clips, straps, and the credit between them
guides.description: How hurricane clips and straps are credited on the wind mitigation form, how inspectors document the connection, and where My Safe Florida Home grants fit.

guides.title: Roof deck attachment and ring-shank nails
guides.description: What roof deck attachment means on the wind mitigation form, why ring-shank nails and nailing pattern matter, and when the credit gets documented.

guides.title: Secondary water resistance: the peel-and-stick credit
guides.description: What secondary water resistance is, why it is usually added during reroofing, and how it is credited and documented on Florida's wind mitigation form.

guides.title: Opening protection and the all-or-nothing rule
guides.description: Impact glass and shutters earn one of the form's major credits, but only if every opening is protected. How the all-or-nothing rule works and what qualifies.

guides.title: My wind mitigation report expired or predates the new form: what to do
guides.description: Wind mitigation reports last up to five years. When an old report still works, when the 2026 form makes a new inspection worth it, and how to decide.
